verse_specific_anchors: the load-bearing tasmāt — the conjunction that ties 2.68 to 2.67 as positive corollary — is explicitly named in the engineering layer's opening 'Therefore — and the conjunction is load-bearing'

the recurring technical term pratiṣṭhitā with cross-verse repetition (2.55, 2.57, 2.58, 2.61, 2.68) is named explicitly as the chapter's defining lexical thread; this is unique to 2.68 because it is the sub-argument's closing restatement

sarvaśaḥ ('on every side / completely') as the totality-qualifier is preserved as the architectural framing 'on every side, sarvaśaḥ' — this specific qualifier is present in 2.68 and not in the surrounding verses' formulations of sense-restraint
objection: The analog could not equally apply to 2.61 (the prior pratiṣṭhitā formulation in the seated-yogi context) — 2.61's engineering analog would lack the post-2.67 restatement function that 2.68's tasmāt structurally requires. Could not apply to 2.55 (Arjuna's question), 2.57 (the self-controlled sage), 2.58 (the tortoise verse with the indriyāṇi-indriya-arthebhyaḥ formulation but a different metaphor). The architected-attention-surface analog with explicit channel-naming and signal-vs-noise distinction is anchored in the verse's specific structural function (post-2.67 restatement of sense-restraint as the condition under which prajñā stabilises). Pass.
